title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR ships auth, admin, and webhooks features but is thoroughly unsafe and should not merge. Security issues alone make it a hard block: production secrets are committed to git (and .gitignore was edited to keep them there), passwords are MD5-hashed with no salt, JWTs have no expiry and no algorithm pin, a hardcoded `admin-master-key` token and an `?adminBypass=1` query parameter create permanent backdoors, full card numbers and CVCs are persisted on the transaction object then dumped to console.log, a SQL-ish eval and a new Function() filter accept arbitrary attacker code from HTTP query params, and webhooks are fired synchronously in the charge hot path to user-supplied URLs with no SSRF protection. Beyond security, the perf story is equally bad: every admin list endpoint calls findAll() with no pagination and filters in JS, the webhook fan-out blocks the payment response, and a 5 MB body limit was silently applied to every route. Both @ts-nocheck files sit on auth and admin logic — exactly where type safety is needed most. Tests assert only toBeDefined() and cover none of the negative paths.

findings.title: Production secrets committed to git history
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY are all in the diff. Once a secret touches git history it is compromised forever — rotating the values is mandatory before anything else. Secrets must live in a secret manager (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, etc.) and be injected at runtime, never stored in the repo. The `pg_live_` prefix on the payment key makes this especially critical: this looks like a live gateway credential.

findings.title: .gitignore edited to actively opt .env into source control
findings.message: The original `.gitignore` entry `.env` was removed and replaced with `!.env` — a negation pattern that explicitly commits the file. This is not a mistake; it is intentional, and that makes it worse. Revert this change, restore `.env` to the ignore list, and rotate every value in the file immediately.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing — trivially reversible
findings.message: `createHash('md5')` with no salt has been broken for decades. Every stored hash is instantly crackable via rainbow tables. Password hashing requires a dedicated KDF with a work factor: `bcrypt` (cost 12+), `argon2id`, or `scrypt`. Replace `hashPassword` and migrate existing hashes on next login.

findings.title: JWT secret falls back to known value; no algorithm pin; no expiry
findings.message: Three independent JWT footguns in one file. (1) `process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'dev-secret-123'` — any misconfigured pod signs tokens with a public string. Use `const SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET; if (!SECRET) throw new Error('JWT_SECRET required');`. (2) `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` has no `algorithm` option — this enables algorithm-confusion attacks including `alg:none`. Pin to `{ algorithm: 'HS256' }` and add `algorithms: ['HS256']` to `verify`. (3) No `expiresIn` — a stolen token is valid indefinitely. Set `expiresIn: '15m'` (access) with a refresh-token flow.

findings.title: Hardcoded admin-master-key backdoor in verifyToken
findings.message: `if (token === 'admin-master-key') return { role: 'admin' }` is a literal hardcoded backdoor. Anyone who reads this diff — or the compiled JS in the container image — now has permanent admin access to production. Delete this unconditionally. There is no staging justification for a backdoor in the token verifier.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 query parameter grants unauthenticated admin access
findings.message: `req.query.adminBypass === '1'` bypasses all authentication and injects `role: admin` into the request. This is a backdoor exploitable by anyone who can send an HTTP request. The comment says "for debugging in staging" — that is not a justification for shipping it in production code, and this file has no environment guard. Delete the entire bypass block.

findings.title: Admin check based on email domain suffix — trivially bypassable
findings.message: `email.endsWith('@admin.com')` is not an identity claim — it is a string check. Anyone who registers with `attacker@admin.com` or modifies a JWT payload (possible if the algorithm isn't pinned) becomes an admin. Authorization must be based on a role stored at registration and enforced in the token, not on an email suffix. The `requireAdmin` middleware should check `req.user?.role === 'admin'`.

findings.title: eval() on user-controlled WHERE clause — Remote Code Execution
findings.message: `evalWhere` builds a JavaScript expression by string-substituting column values into the SQL WHERE clause and calls `eval(expr)`. Any caller that passes attacker-controlled input into `findUserByEmail` or the `listAllUsers(filter)` path reaches this eval. OWASP A03. Delete `FakeDb` entirely — the whole file is three functions that could each be a one-liner operating on a plain array: `users.find(u => u.email === email)`. The SQL-parser abstraction adds nothing and creates RCE.

findings.title: new Function() on HTTP query param — Remote Code Execution
findings.message: `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')` where `filterExpr` comes directly from `req.query.filter`. Any authenticated admin (or anyone who bypasses auth via the mechanisms above) can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the Node process. This is OWASP A03 server-side code injection. Remove the dynamic eval entirely; implement a typed, bounded set of filter parameters (e.g., `?status=succeeded&currency=usd`) with a Zod schema.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored on transaction object and dumped to console.log
findings.message: Two separate PCI-DSS violations in four lines: (1) `card_number: input.card.number` and `cvc: input.card.cvc` are added to the transaction object which is then passed to `this.transactions.save(transaction)` — PAN must never be stored without tokenization/encryption, and CVC must never be stored anywhere, ever (PCI-DSS 3.2.1). (2) `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` immediately serializes the full object including PAN+CVC to stdout. The project already has a pino logger with a redaction allowlist — this console.log bypasses it deliberately. Remove both the storage and the log.

findings.title: card_number column included in CSV export — PAN at rest and in transit
findings.message: The CSV header explicitly includes `card_number` and the mapping at line 50 writes `tx.card_number ?? ''` to every row. This is a downloadable file containing PANs in plaintext — a breach waiting for an S3 presigned URL or a download click. Remove `card_number` from the export entirely; `card_last4` is sufficient for support triage.

findings.title: console.log(req.body) logs plaintext password on every registration
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` prints the user's password to stdout in plaintext. Passwords must never appear in logs. Delete this line. If you need to trace registration flow, log only non-sensitive fields: `logger.debug({ email }, 'register attempt')`.

findings.title: password_hash returned in registration response
findings.message: `res.status(201).json({ ..., user: { ..., password_hash: user.password_hash } })` serializes the password hash to the client. The hash is the crown jewel of the auth system — leaking it lets offline cracking attempts begin immediately. Return only `{ id, email, role }`.

findings.title: Login distinguishes "no such user" (404) from "wrong password" (401) — user enumeration oracle
findings.message: Different status codes and messages for missing-user vs wrong-password let an attacker probe which email addresses are registered. Return a uniform `401 { error: 'invalid credentials' }` for both cases regardless of whether the user exists.

findings.title: Math.random() used for password reset tokens — not a CSPRNG
findings.message: `userId + '-' + Math.random().toString(36) + '-' + Date.now()` is not cryptographically random. `Math.random()` is a PRNG seeded from a predictable state; combined with a guessable userId and a millisecond timestamp, the token space is small enough to brute-force. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` and store only a hashed version server-side.

findings.title: CORS configured with origin: '*' and credentials: true
findings.message: `cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true })` is explicitly rejected by browsers (the spec disallows wildcard origin with credentials), but more importantly it signals intent to expose authenticated responses cross-origin. Use an explicit allowlist: `cors({ origin: ['https://app.honeybook.com'], credentials: true })`. Wildcard + credentials is a well-known misconfiguration that breaks same-origin cookie isolation.

findings.title: SSRF: server-side fetch to user-controlled URL with redirect follow
findings.message: `fetch(hook.url, { redirect: 'follow' })` fires to any URL a user registered. An attacker registers `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS instance metadata), `http://localhost:5432` (internal Postgres), or any RFC-1918 address and gets the response POSTed back to them via redirect chains. Mitigations required before this can ship: validate scheme (https-only), resolve the hostname, block private/loopback/link-local ranges, set `redirect: 'error'`, and enforce a short timeout.

findings.title: @ts-nocheck on the auth user store — type safety disabled where it matters most
findings.message: `// @ts-nocheck` at the top of the file that owns user credentials, password hashes, and SQL-string construction turns off every TypeScript guard. The compiler can't help you catch a mistyped field name or a wrong type flowing into a security-critical function. Same issue in `adminService.ts` line 1. Both files need types, not a compiler gag. If the types are hard to write, the design is telling you something — fix the design (hint: `FakeDb` with a string-eval query engine is the problem in `userStore.ts`; delete it and use a plain array).

findings.title: Module-level mutable singleton — hidden global state that leaks across tests
findings.message: `const webhooks: Webhook[] = []` at module scope is shared by every test run and every `WebhooksService` instance. You can't reset it, can't inject a fake, and can't have two independent instances. The codebase already injects repositories — do the same here: `constructor(private readonly store: Webhook[] = []) {}` or give `WebhooksService` an internal field. Same pattern in `userStore.ts` with `const db = new FakeDb()`.

findings.title: FakeDb SQL-parser abstraction over a plain array — massive over-engineering
findings.message: The entire `FakeDb` class (35 lines), `evalWhere` (15 lines), and a module-level singleton is an elaborate SQL engine for what is, in production, a three-function wrapper around an array. `findUserByEmail` is `users.find(u => u.email === email)`. `createUser` is `users.push(newRow); return newRow`. `listAllUsers` is `[...users]`. The SQL-parser abstraction adds no capability, introduces `eval` RCE, requires `@ts-nocheck`, and buries the actual logic. Delete the whole thing and replace with the three trivial array operations.

findings.title: Admin email-suffix check copy-pasted into every controller method instead of using requireAdmin
findings.message: The four-line admin check (`email.endsWith('@admin.com') || ...`) is duplicated verbatim in `listUsers`, `listTransactions`, `stats`, and `exportCsv`. There is already a `requireAdmin` middleware in `authMiddleware.ts`. Use it on the route level in `routes.ts`: `router.get('/admin/users', authMiddleware, requireAdmin, admin.listUsers)`. Four copies of an authz check means four places to forget the fix when the logic changes — and they already have a bug (the email-suffix check is wrong; see security findings).

findings.title: Webhook fan-out fires synchronously in the charge hot path
findings.message: `await this.webhooks.fireForTransaction(transaction)` blocks the charge response until every registered webhook endpoint responds (or times out). Charge latency now equals `gateway_latency + Σ(latency of every webhook endpoint)`. One slow or hung subscriber stalls a payment. This is one of the classic patterns for turning a payment service into an incident at 2am. Move webhook delivery to a background queue: enqueue a job, return the transaction immediately, deliver out-of-band.

findings.title: fireForTransaction iterates ALL webhooks, not just the transaction owner's
findings.message: `for (const hook of webhooks)` fans out to every registered webhook in the system, not just the ones belonging to the account that triggered the charge. As the webhooks table grows this is O(all webhooks) per charge. The `list()` method already filters by `ownerId` — `fireForTransaction` should do the same. Also, the serial `await fetch` inside the loop means N webhooks = N sequential round trips; use `Promise.all` for parallel delivery.

findings.title: findAll() + in-memory filter on every admin request — O(total transactions) unbounded
findings.message: `const all = await this.transactions.findAll()` loads the entire transactions table into memory before filtering in JS. On 50 seed rows this is fast; on 5M rows this OOMs the pod. Same pattern in `stats()` and `exportTransactionsCsv()`. At minimum: add `limit`/`offset` parameters to all three list endpoints; push the filter predicate down to the repository with an indexed WHERE clause; stream CSV rows in batches rather than buffering the full dataset.

findings.title: JSON body limit raised from 64 KB to 5 MB on every route globally
findings.message: The PR description says this was raised for "large CSV exports / admin payloads" — but CSV is a response, not a request body, and the 5 MB limit now applies to every route including `/charge`, `/auth/register`, and `/auth/login`. This is a cheap DoS amplifier: each request forces the server to parse up to 5 MB of JSON. Revert the global limit to 64 KB; if a specific admin upload route genuinely needs more, scope the override to that single route with a comment explaining the payload it sizes for.

findings.title: exportTransactionsCsv buffers the entire table in memory — latent OOM
findings.message: `findAll()` followed by `.map()` and `.join('\n')` allocates the entire transaction dataset as a string before sending a single byte. For a payment service this will grow without bound. Stream rows in pages using the repository's cursor API and pipe through a transform stream to the response. Also: this is synchronous in-memory work on the hot path — combine with the pagination fix.

findings.title: No schema validation at the auth boundary — raw req.body access
findings.message: `const email = req.body.email; const password = req.body.password` with only a presence check. There is no length limit, no format check, no type enforcement. A Zod schema at the route level is the established pattern in this codebase: `z.object({ email: z.string().email().max(254), password: z.string().min(8).max(128) }).parse(req.body)`. Without it, empty string, null, an object, or a 10 MB password all reach business logic.

findings.title: Password reset token returned in HTTP response body and logged to console
findings.message: The reset token is both returned directly in `res.json({ ok: true, resetToken })` and printed to `console.log`. Tokens must be delivered out-of-band (email) and never returned in the API response — returning them in the response means the token is only as secret as the TLS session and any reverse proxy log. Additionally, `console.log` with PII (the email address) and the token bypasses pino's redaction.

findings.title: stats() response uses snake_case keys — inconsistent with the camelCase API
findings.message: `users_count`, `transactions_count`, `total_volume` break the API's established camelCase contract (`cardLast4`, `customerId`, `createdAt`). Every consumer of this endpoint will need a special case. Use `usersCount`, `transactionsCount`, `totalVolume`. The same mixing appears in `UserRow` (`password_hash`, `created_at`) leaking into API responses via `authController` line 31.

findings.title: Auth tests assert only token isDefined — prove nothing about correctness
findings.message: `expect(res.body.token).toBeDefined()` passes whether the token is a valid JWT, an empty string, `null`, or the literal string `'admin-master-key'`. These tests would survive every regression they were supposed to catch. Assert the actual contract: decode the token and check `sub`/`email`/`role`; assert `res.body.user` shape without `password_hash`; assert that a duplicate-email registration returns 409; assert that a wrong password returns 401 not 404. The negative paths — which are exactly the security-critical ones — have zero coverage.

findings.title: console.log throughout webhooksService instead of injected logger — also leaks PAN
findings.message: `console.log('firing webhooks for', tx.id, 'payload:', payload)` serializes the full transaction JSON — which after paymentsService changes now contains `card_number` and `cvc` — bypassing pino's redaction allowlist. Use `logger.info({ transactionId: tx.id }, 'firing webhooks')`. Same pattern on lines 38 and 42. There are no `console.*` calls in a finished diff; every one here escaped code review.
